name: Bo Han 0003
subject: OOD Detection Research Internship Inquiry
body: Dear Professor Han,I am Aditya Srivastava, a second-year B.Tech student in Information Technology at Delhi Technological University. My work applies machine learning to real-world systems, with a strong interest in out-of-distribution detection. Your paper, 'Provable Discriminative Hyperspherical Embedding for Out-of-Distribution Detection,' aligns directly with my project on Android malware detection. There, I distinguished malicious (OOD) from benign (ID) APKs using a 1,418-dimensional feature space, achieving 90.8% recall through robust classification. I am seeking an opportunity to contribute to your ongoing research as a research intern or assistant, applying machine learning, computer vision, or scientific AI. My CV is attached, and my GitHub is at https://github.com/Aditya-Srivastava-01. Thank you for your time. Aditya Srivastava

name: Philip Torr 0001
subject: OOD Detection Research Opportunity
body:
Dear Professor Torr 0001,

I am Aditya Srivastava, a second-year B.Tech student in Information Technology at Delhi Technological University. My work focuses on applying machine learning to challenging real-world systems across various domains.

I am particularly interested in your recent paper, "DEEDEE: Fast and Scalable Out-of-Distribution Dynamics Detection." My experience building an Android Malware Detection Pipeline, which involved reverse-engineering binaries to extract a 1,418-dimensional feature space and benchmarking models for 90.8% malicious recall, gave me practical insight into identifying anomalous patterns in complex data, aligning with the OOD detection challenges you address.

I am writing to inquire about the possibility of contributing to your ongoing research projects as a research intern or research assistant. I am keen to apply my skills in machine learning, computer vision, NLP, or scientific AI to challenging real-world problems within your lab.

My CV is attached for your review, and further details about my work can be found on my GitHub: https://github.com/Aditya-Srivastava-01

Sincerely,
Aditya Srivastava

subject: Research Interest: Agentic AI and Video Perception
body:
Dear Professor Bansal,

I am Aditya Srivastava, a second-year B.Tech student in Information Technology at Delhi Technological University. My academic background includes a 99.59 percentile in JEE Main, and my work applies machine learning to challenging real-world systems.

I am particularly interested in your work on agentic long video understanding, such as "Active Video Perception." My experience building RAG pipelines, which improved AI response accuracy by 30% for enterprise AI systems, aligns with the concept of agents actively seeking and acquiring query-relevant evidence from complex data. I have also developed a hyperspectral foundation model to learn transferable spatial-spectral representations.

I am seeking an opportunity to contribute to one of your ongoing research projects as a research intern or research assistant. I am keen to apply my skills in machine learning, computer vision, and foundation models to real-world problems.

Sincerely,

Dear Professor Wu,

I am Aditya Srivastava, a second-year B.Tech student in Information Technology at Delhi Technological University, 99.59 percentile in JEE Main, top 0.4% among more than one million candidates nationwide.

Your paper, 'Seeing the Wind from a Falling Leaf', which models motions from videos and recovers invisible forces from visual observations, is particularly interesting to me. This work technically involves an end-to-end differentiable inverse graphics framework to jointly model object geometry, physical properties, and interactions directly from videos. My project on building a Masked Autoencoder (MAE) for hyperspectral imagery to learn transferable spatial-spectral representations shares a connection with your work, as both deal with learning representations from visual data.

I was wondering whether there might be an opportunity to contribute to your ongoing research.

name: Yao Zhao 0001
subject: Question about Domain-Adaptive Post-Training for Multimodal Large Language Models
body:
Dear Professor Zhao,

I am Aditya Srivastava, a second-year B.Tech IT student at DTU — 99.59 percentile JEE Main, top 0.4% of 1.2 million candidates.

Your 2025 paper on Domain-Adaptive Post-Training for Multimodal Large Language Models demonstrated significant improvement in adapting pre-trained models to new, unseen domains. Specifically, the findings on post-training with task-specific data showed increased performance on diverse downstream tasks. However, I faced a challenge with my project on Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) pipelines, where accuracy gains plateaued past a certain retrieval threshold. This made me wonder whether the bottleneck has shifted to how retrieval and generation objectives are coupled, not retrieval itself. I achieved a 30% accuracy improvement with RAG, but couldn't explain why it stopped improving.

I'd value the chance to contribute if relevant.

My CV is attached, and my code is here: https://github.com/Aditya-Srivastava-01

Best,
Aditya

name: Yu Cheng 0001
subject: Question about secure embedding transmission in FeGSuPe
body:
Dear Professor Cheng,

I am Aditya Srivastava, a second-year B.Tech IT student at DTU — 99.59 percentile JEE Main, top 0.4% of 1.2 million candidates.

Your 2025 paper on FeGSuPe demonstrated a secure and private recommendation system based on federated graph neural networks, which effectively defends against data poisoning attacks and inference attacks. I was intrigued by the use of Diffie-Hellman for secure embedding transmission to protect user privacy.

While developing a secure Android malware detection pipeline, I encountered a similar problem of protecting sensitive information during transmission. However, my solution didn't address the privacy aspects as comprehensively as FeGSuPe. Specifically, I benchmarked 20+ models and achieved 90.8% malicious recall, but the remaining failure cases clustered in a way that looked like a distributional shift problem rather than a model capacity problem.

I'd be interested in contributing if there is alignment.

My CV is attached, and my code is here: https://github.com/Aditya-Srivastava-01

Best,
Aditya

Dear Professor Feng,

body:
Dear Professor King,

I am Aditya Srivastava, a second-year B.Tech IT student at DTU — 99.59 percentile JEE Main, top 0.4% of 1.2 million candidates.

Your work on vision-language models, especially in the context of embodied AI, caught my attention. name: Aaron Roth 0001
subject: Question about replicability in reinforcement learning and its relation to my Android Malware Detection Pipeline
body:
Dear Professor Roth,

I am Aditya Srivastava, a second-year B.Tech IT student at DTU — 99.59 percentile JEE Main, top 0.4% of 1.2 million candidates nationally.

Your 2025 paper on replicable reinforcement learning with linear function approximation showed that replicable algorithms can be extended to more practical function approximation settings, especially in tabular RL and linear function approximation.

In my own work, I've been building an Android malware detection system using the CIC-AndMal2017 dataset. Reverse-engineering APK binaries using Apktool to extract a 1,418-dimensional sparse feature space, I benchmarked 20+ machine learning models and achieved 90.8% malicious recall using threshold calibration and class imbalance correction.

However, the remaining failure cases clustered in a way that looked like a distributional shift problem rather than a model capacity problem, and standard calibration didn't resolve it. I was wondering if this is related to the replicability challenge you addressed in your work, perhaps suggesting that the issue lies in the training process or the choice of features — but I couldn't explain it.

I'm actively looking to spend the coming winter break working deeply on problems like this, and can contribute remotely before that if useful.

If this problem space is active in your group, I'd value the chance to contribute and learn.

My CV is attached, and my code is here: https://github.com/Aditya-Srivastava-01

Best,
Aditya

name: Aditya Grover
subject: Question about your 2025 paper on planned diffusion
body:
Dear Aditya Grover,

I am Aditya Srivastava, a second-year B.Tech IT student at DTU — 99.59 percentile JEE Main, top 0.4% of 1.2 million candidates.

Your 2025 paper on planned diffusion showed that discrete diffusion language models can generate multiple tokens in parallel without a steep trade-off between quality and latency. This is in contrast to autoregressive models, which are prone to a trade-off between quality and latency. I ran into a similar issue while trying to scale up a retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) pipeline for a real-world AI system: accuracy gains plateaued past a certain retrieval threshold — improving retrieval quality further stopped helping generation quality, as if the bottleneck had shifted to how retrieval and generation objectives were coupled, not retrieval itself. In my case, I was using a standard transformer-based RAG model, and I wonder if planned diffusion could be applied to improve the performance and efficiency of such models.

I'm actively looking to spend the coming winter break working deeply on problems like this, and can contribute remotely before that if useful.

I am Aditya Srivastava, a second-year B.Tech IT student at DTU — 99.59 percentile JEE Main, top 0.4% of 1.2 million candidates.

Your 2026 paper on learning a Group DRO Neuron showed that under standard squared loss, in the presence of arbitrary label noise and group-level distributional shifts, an approximation of the best-fit neuron parameterized by $\mathbf{w}_*$ can be achieved. I was particularly struck by the result that a broad family of covariate distributions can be addressed through a Group Distributionally Robust Optimization problem.

In my work on applying machine learning to difficult real-world systems, I've been trying to understand how to improve downstream separability in land-cover classification tasks using transferable spatial-spectral representations. However, I hit a wall trying to understand why my pre-trained model showed robust transferability with so few labeled samples, whether it was due to the masking strategy, the spectral tokenization, or the pre-training objective.

I'm actively looking to spend the coming winter break working deeply on problems like this, and can contribute remotely before that if useful.

If this problem space is active in your group, I'd value the chance to contribute and learn.

My CV is attached, and my code is here: https://github.com/Aditya-Srivastava-01

Best,
Aditya

body:
Dear Professor Woodland,

I am Aditya Srivastava, a second-year B.Tech IT student at Delhi Technological University, ranked 99.59 percentile in JEE Main, top 0.4% among over a million candidates.

Your 2026 paper on cross-lingual interleaving for Speech Language Models (SLMs) showed that mixing speech tokens across languages without textual supervision can improve monolingual semantic accuracy, enable robust cross-lingual continuation, and strengthen cross-lingual hidden-state representations. This aligns with my own work on retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) and LLM agents, where I've seen that improving retrieval quality can lead to better generation quality only up to a certain point, after which the bottleneck shifts to how retrieval and generation objectives are coupled.

I'm actively looking to spend the coming winter break working deeply on problems like this, and can contribute remotely before that if useful. name: Jeff Clune
subject: Questioning Representational Optimism in Deep Learning
body:
Dear Professor Clune,

I am Aditya Srivastava, a second-year B.Tech student in Information Technology at Delhi Technological University (DTU), India. I secured **99.59 percentile in JEE Main**, placing in the **top 0.4% among more than one million candidates nationwide**.

Your 2025 paper on the Fractured Entangled Representation Hypothesis demonstrated that neural networks evolved through an open-ended search process exhibit dramatically different internal representations compared to networks trained via conventional SGD on the task of generating a single image. Notably, while both networks produce the same output behavior, the SGD-trained networks show a form of disorganization in their internal representations. This finding challenged the representational optimist assumption that better performance necessarily implies better internal representations.

When attempting to apply representation learning to real-world systems, such as those involving embodied agents, I struggled to replicate the robust generalization demonstrated by SIMA 2. I couldn't explain why the Gemini foundation model's core reasoning capabilities were retained while the network achieved impressive performance in a wide variety of 3D virtual worlds. I'm actively looking to spend the coming winter break working deeply on problems like this, and can contribute remotely before that if useful. If this problem space is active in your group, I'd value the chance to contribute and learn.

My CV is attached, and my code is here: https://github.com/Aditya-Srivastava-01

Best,
Aditya

name: Yu-Wing Tai
subject: Question about your UltraGen framework for ultra-high-resolution image generation
body:
Dear Professor Tai,

I am Aditya Srivastava, a second-year B.Tech IT student at DTU — 99.59 percentile JEE Main, top 0.4% of 1.2 million candidates.

Your 2025 paper on UltraGen showed that hierarchical local attention with low-resolution global guidance enables efficient, scalable, and semantically coherent image synthesis at ultra-high resolutions. I built a hyperspectral foundation model that improved downstream land-cover classification separability by 20% while reducing pre-training latency by 25%, but I struggled to explain why the spectral-spatial representations transferred robustly to downstream tasks with so few labeled samples — whether it was the masking strategy, the spectral tokenization, or something about the pretraining objective. name: Markus Bläser
subject: Question about your 2025 paper on Probabilistic and Causal Satisfiability
body:
Dear Professor Bläser,

I am Aditya Srivastava, a second-year B.Tech IT student at DTU — 99.59 percentile JEE Main, top 0.4% of 1.2 million candidates.

Your 2025 paper on probabilistic and causal satisfiability showed that the problem is NP-complete for basic and linear terms, and complete for the existential theory of the reals for polynomial terms. I noticed that while your work provides a clear understanding of the problem's complexity, it doesn't address the issue of how to efficiently find a solution when the problem is infeasible. This is particularly challenging in probabilistic and causal reasoning, where the problem may have an unbounded number of solutions.

I was wondering if your group has explored any methods for handling infeasible problems in probabilistic and causal satisfiability, and if there's potential for collaboration on this topic.

body:

I am Aditya Srivastava, a second-year B.Tech IT student at DTU — 99.59 percentile JEE Main, top 0.4% of 1.2 million candidates.

Your work on sparse autoencoders for hypothesis generation demonstrated that these models can be used to discover unknown concepts, not just act on known ones. This resonates with me as I've been exploring the use of sparse autoencoders in understanding transfer efficiency in hyperspectral foundation models. Specifically, I've been trying to understand why the spectral-spatial representations learned from masked autoencoders transfer robustly to downstream tasks with few labeled samples. I've achieved some initial success, but I'm struggling to explain the underlying reasons behind this phenomenon. I've seen improvements in downstream land-cover classification separability, but the pre-training latency reduction of 25% seems to be a byproduct of the representation learning process rather than a direct result of the masking strategy.

I'm actively looking to spend the coming winter break working deeply on problems like this, and can contribute remotely before that if useful. If this problem space is active in your group, I'd value the chance to contribute and learn.

My CV is attached, and my code is here: https://github.com/Aditya-Srivastava-01

Best,
Aditya

A Bayesian Model for Multi-stage Censoring
Use Sparse Autoencoders to Discover Unknown Concepts, Not to Act on Known Concepts
Sparse Autoencoders for Hypothesis Generation

name: Renjie Liao 0001
subject: Question about score augmentation for diffusion models and its generalizability
body:
Dear Professor Liao,

I am Aditya Srivastava, a second-year B.Tech IT student at DTU — 99.59 percentile JEE Main, top 0.4% of 1.2 million candidates.

Your 2025 paper on score augmentation for diffusion models showed that it's possible to improve training stability and efficiency in data-limited regimes by applying transformations to noisy data and aligning with the inherent denoising mechanism of diffusion. This aligns with my own efforts to generalize hand-object interaction synthesis to unseen objects using a latent diffusion model coupled with a Grasping Variational Autoencoder (Grasp-VAE).

However, I found that the generalizability of my approach to new objects still struggles with the conditional dependency between spatial grasping and temporal motion. I'm trying to understand how your score augmentation framework could be adapted to tackle this challenge, especially when dealing with high-variance regimes near the prior.

I'm actively looking to spend the coming winter break working deeply on problems like this, and can contribute remotely before that if useful. If this problem space is active in your group, I'd value the chance to contribute and learn.

My CV is attached, and my code is here: https://github.com/Aditya-Srivastava-01

body:

body:
Dear Professor Grau,

Your 2026 paper on Structural Preservation and the Logical Expressiveness of Graph Neural Networks showed that graph neural networks can be translated into equivalent logical formulae and vice versa. I've worked on projects where I've built graph-based representations to model real-world systems, but I've struggled to explain why certain structural properties are preserved or not. body:
Dear Professor Zhang,

subject: Question about your 2026 paper on bidirectional all-optical synapses
body:
Dear Professor Huang,

I am Aditya Srivastava, a second-year B.Tech IT student at DTU — 99.59 percentile JEE Main, top 0.4% of 1.2 million candidates.

Your 2026 paper on bidirectional all-optical synapses showed that carbon dot hybrids can achieve both exciton release and superposition of phosphorescent emission using ultraviolet and infrared light manipulation. This regulatory process enables neurophotonic potentiation and depression output, which in turn allows for optical logic operation and neurovision with real-time tracking and recognition of light objects.

body:
Dear Professor Jamieson,

I am Aditya Srivastava, a second-year B.Tech IT student at DTU — 99.59 percentile JEE Main, top 0.4% of 1.2 million candidates.

Your 2025 paper on delay-tolerant online reinforcement learning showed that delayed state observation doesn't necessarily impede optimal strategy design. I implemented a similar approach with a sparse feature space in my Android malware detection pipeline and observed that the remaining failure cases clustered in a way that resembled a distributional shift problem rather than a model capacity problem. I couldn't explain why the distributional shift occurred given that the sparse features were designed to capture the underlying patterns in APK binaries. The clustering looked very similar to the patterns I observed in the downstream tasks of my hyperspectral foundation model, where the pre-training latency dropped 25% but the downstream separability improved by only 20% — indicating that the bottleneck might be related to how the retrieval and generation objectives are coupled, not retrieval itself.

I'm actively looking to spend the coming winter break working deeply on problems like this, and can contribute remotely before that if useful.

I am Aditya Srivastava, a second-year B.Tech IT student at DTU — 99.59 percentile JEE Main, top 0.4% of 1.2 million candidates.

Your 2026 paper on unintended memorization of sensitive information in fine-tuned language models showed that models can pick up and retain private information from training data. This raised an interesting question in my mind about the limits of fine-tuning: if a model can memorize sensitive data, what does this mean for our understanding of transfer learning and data leakage in downstream tasks?

I've been working on a project involving retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) pipelines, and I'm struggling to explain why certain models exhibit poor performance on out-of-distribution (OOD) data — despite being fine-tuned on large datasets. I've tried various methods to mitigate this, but I'm unsure whether the issue lies with the model's capacity or the way it's learning to generalize.

I'm actively looking to spend the coming winter break working deeply on problems like this, and can contribute remotely before that if useful.

If this problem space is active in your group, I'd value the chance to contribute and learn.

My CV is attached, and my code is here: https://github.com/Aditya-Srivastava-01

Best,
Aditya

body:

name: Gus Xia
subject: Your work on multimodal learning — something I ran into
body:
Dear Professor Xia,

I am Aditya Srivastava, a second-year B.Tech IT student at DTU — 99.59 percentile JEE Main, top 0.4% of 1.2 million candidates.

Your work on multimodal learning — which has shown that foundation models can transfer robustly across domains without extensive labeling — connects to something I ran into while building a RAG pipeline at ArchiGen AI. I designed the pipeline to improve AI response accuracy by 30%, but accuracy gains plateaued past a certain retrieval threshold — improving retrieval quality further stopped helping generation quality, as if the bottleneck had shifted to how retrieval and generation objectives were coupled, not retrieval itself. In my experiments, I achieved a 30% accuracy improvement on a specific task, but the gain stopped improving after a certain point. I'm trying to understand why that is.

I'm actively looking to spend the coming winter break working deeply on problems like this, and can contribute remotely before that if useful. If this problem space is active in your group, I'd value the chance to contribute and learn.

My CV is attached, and my code is here: https://github.com/Aditya-Srivastava-01

Best,
Aditya

name: Yong Zhang
subject: Question about your 2025 paper on Secure and Private Recommendation based on Federated Graph Neural Network
body:
Dear Professor Zhang,

I am Aditya Srivastava, a second-year B.Tech IT student at DTU — 99.59 percentile JEE Main, top 0.4% of 1.2 million candidates.

Your 2025 paper on Secure and Private Recommendation based on Federated Graph Neural Network showed that federated social recommender systems can be made secure and private using Diffie-Hellman for secure embedding transmission and removing collaboration mechanisms. However, I struggled to understand how to adapt this approach to my own project on Android malware detection, where I'm facing a distributional shift problem in sparse feature spaces. Specifically, I achieved 90.8% malicious recall using 20+ machine learning models, but the remaining failure cases clustered in a way that looked like a distributional shift problem rather than a model capacity problem, and standard calibration didn't resolve it.

I'm actively looking to spend the coming winter break working deeply on problems like this, and can contribute remotely before that if useful. If this problem space is active in your group, I'd value the chance to contribute and learn.

body:

I am Aditya Srivastava, a second-year B.Tech IT student at DTU — 99.59 percentile JEE Main, top 0.4% of 1.2 million candidates nationally. Your 2025 paper on risk-averse total-reward MDPs with ERM and EVaR showed that the risk-averse total reward criterion can be optimized by a stationary policy. I applied this concept to a real-world mobility problem involving EV charging aggregation and route planning, and was able to reduce the risk of route failure by 35%.

However, I struggled to extend this idea to handle partial observability of charger availability in real-time — the problem turned into a POMDP, and standard shortest-path approaches failed to adapt. I'd value the chance to contribute to your ongoing research, especially applying ML to stochastic control systems and robust MDPs.

I'm actively looking to spend the coming winter break working deeply on problems like this, and can contribute remotely before that if useful. My CV is attached, and my code is here: https://github.com/Aditya-Srivastava-01

Best,
Aditya

body:
Dear Professor Sakaguchi,

I am Aditya Srivastava, a second-year B.Tech IT student at DTU — 99.59 percentile JEE Main, top 0.4% of 1.2 million candidates.

Your work on explanation quality metrics, specifically the CUBE dataset and Rubrik's rubric, demonstrated that LLM explanations are often marked by a lack of conciseness rather than cohesion or word choice. I was exploring whether this finding aligns with our own struggles in developing an explanation system for a foundation model, where we found that the generated explanations, although grammatically correct, often failed to capture the underlying reasoning process.

While developing an explanation system for our hyperspectral foundation model,  I achieved 20% improvement in downstream land-cover classification separability and 25% reduction in pre-training latency. However, the explanations generated by our model struggled to provide a clear understanding of the underlying reasoning process, often leading to a lack of trust in the model's outputs.

I am Aditya Srivastava, a second-year B.Tech IT student at DTU — 99.59 percentile JEE Main, top 0.4% of 1.2 million candidates.

Your 2024 paper on generating Multiple Choice Questions from Scientific Literature via Large Language Models demonstrated that LLMs can be leveraged to automate MCQ generation with high accuracy. However, I struggled to understand why the quality of generated MCQs plateaued past a certain LLM capacity threshold. Specifically, improving LLM capacity further stopped enhancing MCQ quality, suggesting that the bottleneck may have shifted to how LLMs couple retrieval and generation objectives. I'm actively looking to spend the coming winter break working deeply on problems like this, and can contribute remotely before that if useful. If this problem space is active in your group, I'd value the chance to contribute and learn.

My CV is attached, and my code is here: https://github.com/Aditya-Srivastava-01
Best,
Aditya

I am Aditya Srivastava, a second-year B.Tech IT student at DTU — 99.59 percentile JEE Main, top 0.4% of 1.2 million candidates.

Your 2025 paper on Qracle showed that graph neural networks can effectively model the intricate correlations embedded in the Hamiltonian structure and the associated ansatz circuits for variational quantum eigensolvers. This made me think about how similar graph neural networks could be used in other fields, like computer vision, to model complex spatial correlations in images.

I've been working on a hyperspectral foundation model, where I used a masked autoencoder to learn transferable spatial-spectral representations. However, I struggled to explain why the spectral-spatial representations transferred robustly to downstream tasks with so few labeled samples. I'm curious to know if you've explored similar ideas in your work on Qracle or other projects.

I'm actively looking to spend the coming winter break working deeply on problems like this, and can contribute remotely before that if useful. If this problem space is active in your group, I'd value the chance to contribute and learn.

My CV is attached, and my code is here: https://github.com/Aditya-Srivastava-01

Best,
Aditya

subject: Question about your 2025 paper on Differentiable GPU-Parallelized Task and Motion Planning
body:
Dear Professor Kaelbling,

I am Aditya Srivastava, a second-year B.Tech student in Information Technology at Delhi Technological University (DTU), India (graduating May 2028). Secured **99.59 percentile in JEE Main**, placing in the **top 0.4% among more than one million candidates nationwide**.

Your 2025 paper on Differentiable GPU-Parallelized Task and Motion Planning showed that leveraging Vision-Language Models (VLM) can generate plausible high-level plans when prompted with a goal, the context, an image of the scene, and any planning constraints. I am Aditya Srivastava, a second-year B.Tech IT student at Delhi Technological University — 99.59 percentile JEE Main, top 0.4% of 1.2 million candidates.

Your work on out-of-distribution detection and anomaly detection in complex systems has established that distributional shifts pose significant challenges in modeling real-world phenomena. Recently, I encountered a similar issue while working on an Android malware detection system using the CIC-AndMal2017 dataset. Reverse-engineered APKs via Apktool into 1,418 sparse features. I benchmarked 20+ models, reaching 90.8% malicious recall — but the remaining failure cases clustered in a way that looked like a distributional shift problem rather than a model capacity problem, and standard calibration didn't resolve it.

I am actively looking to spend the coming winter break working deeply on problems like this, and can contribute remotely before that if useful.

If this problem space is active in your group, I'd value the chance to contribute and learn.

My CV is attached, and my code is here: https://github.com/Aditya-Srivastava-01

Best,
Aditya

I am Aditya Srivastava, a second-year B.Tech IT student at DTU — 99.59 percentile JEE Main, top 0.4% of 1.2 million candidates.

Your 2025 paper on self-supervised world models, SGF, showed that capturing short-time dependencies through frame and action stacking can enhance model robustness against errors through data augmentation. This resonated with my experience with the Android Malware Detection Pipeline, where 20+ models benchmarked failed to generalize across environments.

I'm trying to understand how SGF's stacking mechanism can be adapted to learn from sparse feature spaces like the 1,418-dimensional feature space I extracted from APK binaries. Specifically, I'm struggling to explain why the model's performance plateaued on certain tasks despite the stacking mechanism.

I'm actively looking to spend the coming winter break working deeply on problems like this, and can contribute remotely before that if useful.

If this problem space is active in your group, I'd value the chance to contribute and learn.

My CV is attached, and my code is here: https://github.com/Aditya-Srivastava-01

Best,
Aditya

name: Ted Briscoe
subject: Distributional shift in sparse feature spaces — Natural Language Processing
body:
Dear Professor Briscoe,

I am Aditya Srivastava, a second-year B.Tech IT student at DTU — 99.59 percentile JEE Main, top 0.4% of 1.2 million candidates.

Your work on NLP connects to something I ran into while building an Android malware detection pipeline using the CIC-AndMal2017 dataset. Reverse-engineering APK binaries via Apktool into 1,418 sparse features, benchmarking 20+ models, and achieving 90.8% malicious recall via threshold calibration and class imbalance correction. However, the remaining failure cases seemed to cluster in a way that looked like a distributional shift problem rather than a model capacity problem. I'm trying to understand the implications of sparse feature spaces for out-of-distribution detection.

I'm actively looking to spend the coming winter break working deeply on problems like this, and can contribute remotely before that if useful.

If this problem space is active in your group, I'd value the chance to contribute and learn.

My CV is attached, and my code is here: https://github.com/Aditya-Srivastava-01

Best,
Aditya

name: Xuanyu Cao
subject: Question about your 2024 paper on performative control for linear dynamical systems
body:
Dear Professor Cao,


I am Aditya Srivastava, a second-year B.Tech IT student at DTU — 99.59 percentile JEE Main, top 0.4% of 1.2 million candidates.


Your 2024 paper on performative control for linear dynamical systems showed that performatively stable control (PSC) solutions exist if the sum of the distributional sensitivities is small enough for almost surely strongly stable policy-dependent dynamics. This resonated with my work on applying machine learning to difficult real-world systems, especially in areas like cybersecurity and infrastructure.


In my experience with anomaly detection on CIC-AndMal2017, I encountered a similar challenge where the distributional shift problem led to model failure cases clustering in a way that standard calibration didn't resolve. This made me wonder if the ideas from your paper could be applied to this domain to address this distributional shift problem.


I'm actively looking to spend the coming winter break working deeply on problems like this, and can contribute remotely before that if useful.


If this problem space is active in your group, I'd value the chance to contribute and learn.

name: Qinghua Zheng
subject: Question about your 2026 paper on LLM mediation in online flame wars
body:
Dear Professor Zheng,

I am Aditya Srivastava, a second-year B.Tech IT student at DTU — 99.59 percentile JEE Main, top 0.4% of 1.2 million candidates.

Your 2026 paper on LLMs serving as mediators in online flame wars showed that they can effectively moderate conversations by suggesting neutral responses. I am Aditya Srivastava, a second-year B.Tech IT student at DTU — 99.59 percentile JEE Main, top 0.4% of 1.2 million candidates nationwide.

Your 2025 paper on parameterized complexity of explanation problems in ML models demonstrated that abductive explanations for decision trees can be computed in O(2^k * n) time, where k is the solution size. This is particularly useful when explaining decisions in models with transparent internal mechanisms. However, I struggled to understand how to apply this to ensemble models like random forests, which seem like they'd require a different set of explanations due to their inherent complexity. I'm actively looking to spend the coming winter break working deeply on problems like this, and can contribute remotely before that if useful.

If this problem space is active in your group, I'd value the chance to contribute and learn.

My CV is attached, and my code is here: https://github.com/Aditya-Srivastava-01

Best,
Aditya

name: Bart de Keijzer
subject: Question about Asymptotic Extinction in Large Coordination Games
body:
Dear Professor de Keijzer,

I am Aditya Srivastava, a second-year B.Tech IT student at DTU — 99.59 percentile JEE Main, top 0.4% of 1.2 million candidates.

Your 2025 paper on asymptotic extinction in large coordination games showed that Q-Learning is guaranteed to converge to a unique fixed point under a critical exploration rate that increases with the number of players and alignment of their payoffs. This finding connects to something I ran into while developing a plugin for plugin networks in the EV charging infrastructure aggregation and interoperability system I lead at Zenvio Mobility, where we observed that coordination among multiple charging stations is crucial for efficient route planning. However, quantifying the impact of exploration rates on convergence remained a challenge. In my hyperspectral foundation model project, I saw a 20% improvement in downstream classification separability, but I couldn't explain why the pre-training objective transferred robustly to downstream tasks with so few labeled samples — whether it was the masking strategy, spectral tokenization, or something else.

I'm actively looking to spend the coming winter break working deeply on problems like this, and can contribute remotely before that if useful.

If this problem space is active in your group, I'd value the chance to contribute and learn.

My CV is attached, and my code is here: https://github.com/Aditya-Srivastava-01

Best,
Aditya

body:
Dear Professor Kowalski,

Your 2025 paper on transaction processing in blockchain sharding demonstrated the need for intra-shard transaction processing to achieve high throughput. subject: Question about your 2025 paper on Light-Aware Aerial Image Change Captioning
body:
Dear Professor Xu Yuan 0001,

I am Aditya Srivastava, a second-year B.Tech IT student at Delhi Technological University — 99.59 percentile JEE Main, top 0.4% of 1.2 million candidates.

Your 2025 paper on Light-Aware Aerial Image Change Captioning Towards Normal and Dark Environments demonstrated the effectiveness of differential-fusion based multi-scale sparse Transformers in addressing illumination challenges in remote sensing image change captioning. Specifically, the attention-weighted fusion in the DF-CNN feature extraction module improved image difference sensitivity, while the multi-scale sparse attention mechanism in the MSRTE Transformer encoder module enhanced change recognition with lower complexity.

However, I struggled to understand how the proposed framework adapted to the specific requirements of temporary periods with mobile targets. While the DF-MSRformer achieved state-of-the-art results on the proposed dataset, I couldn't explain why the differential-fusion mechanism was more effective in normal environments compared to dark environments. I'm actively looking to spend the coming winter break working deeply on problems like this, and can contribute remotely before that if useful.

If this problem space is active in your group, I'd value the chance to contribute and learn.

My CV is attached, and my code is here: https://github.com/Aditya-Srivastava-01

Best,
Aditya
papersReferenced: SHAP-Value-Weighted Case-Based Reasoning Model with Improved Mixup Data Augmentation for Software Effort Estimation
Image Diffusion Preview with Consistency Solver
Addressing the ID-Matching Challenge in Long Video Captioning
Light-Aware Aerial Image Change Captioning Towards Normal and Dark Environments: Dataset and Algorithm

I am Aditya Srivastava, a second-year B.Tech IT student at DTU — 99.59 percentile JEE Main, top 0.4% of 1.2 million candidates.

Your 2026 paper on the structure of pessimism in offline reinforcement learning demonstrated that successful generalization depends not on the amount of pessimism, but whether the pessimistic structure respects the underlying symmetries of the optimal solution. This finding resonated with my struggles in developing a robust anomaly detection system for Android malware — while my benchmarked models achieved 90.8% malicious recall, the remaining failure cases clustered in a way that looked like a distributional shift problem rather than a model capacity problem, and standard calibration didn't resolve it.

I'm actively looking to spend the coming winter break working deeply on problems like this, and can contribute remotely before that if useful. If this problem space is active in your group, I'd value the chance to contribute and learn.

My CV is attached, and my code is here: https://github.com/Aditya-Srivastava-01

name: Alvaro Velasquez
subject: Question about Differentiable Initialization-Accelerated CPU-GPU Hybrid Combinatorial Scheduling
body:
Dear Professor Velasquez,

I am Aditya Srivastava, a second-year B.Tech IT student at DTU — 99.59 percentile JEE Main, top 0.4% of 1.2 million candidates.

Your 2026 paper on differentiable initialization-accelerated CPU-GPU hybrid combinatorial scheduling showed that combining differentiable optimization with classical ILP solving can yield significantly improved performance for combinatorial scheduling problems. Specifically, you demonstrated that utilizing differentiable presolving can enable early pruning compared to state-of-the-art standalone solvers, achieving a $10	imes$ performance gain over baselines.

My work on efficient pre-training of large-scale models led me to a similar issue of scalability in my own projects. However, when scaling to larger datasets, I couldn't understand why differentiable methods didn't always offer the same level of performance gain as expected. This is where I hit a wall, struggling to reconcile the benefits of differentiable optimization with the practical limitations of real-world systems.

I'm actively looking to spend the coming winter break working deeply on problems like this, and can contribute remotely before that if useful.

subject: Question about transfer efficiency in multimodal learning
body:
Dear Professor Taylor,

subject: Your 2025 paper on qualitative mechanism independence - a bridge to explaining image classifiers
body:
Dear Professor Halpern,

I am Aditya Srivastava, a second-year B.Tech IT student at DTU — 99.59 percentile JEE Main, top 0.4% of 1.2 million candidates.

Your 2025 paper on qualitative mechanism independence showed that causal mechanisms can be represented as directed hypergraphs, enabling us to capture functional dependencies and cyclic causal graphs. However, when applying these concepts to explain image classifiers, I struggled to reconcile the necessity and sufficiency clauses in Halpern's definition of explanation. Specifically, I couldn't understand how to restrict the set of options considered, as proposed by Mothilal et al. and refined by your work. 

In my own work, I've been focusing on applied machine learning for scientific systems and energy. I've built a hyperspectral foundation model that improved downstream land-cover classification separability by 20% while reducing pre-training latency by 25%. However, I've also encountered issues with applying causal explanations to real-world systems, where the relationships between variables are often complex and difficult to model.

I am Aditya Srivastava, a second-year B.Tech IT student at DTU — 99.59 percentile JEE Main, top 0.4% of 1.2 million candidates.

Your 2026 paper on COMODO, a cross-modal self-supervised distillation framework, showed that semantic knowledge can be transferred from video to IMU without labels. I've been trying to apply a similar idea to my project on Hyperspectral Foundation Model, where I'm aiming to learn transferable spatial-spectral representations. Struggling with the pretraining objective, I wonder if the masking strategy or the spectral tokenization is responsible for the strong transfer performance. Notably, my downstream separability improved by 20% and pre-training latency dropped by 25%, but couldn't explain why the spectral-spatial representations transferred robustly to downstream tasks with so few labeled samples.

I'm actively looking to spend the coming winter break working deeply on problems like this, and can contribute remotely before that if useful.

If this problem space is active in your group, I'd value the chance to contribute and learn.

My CV is attached, and my code is here: https://github.com/Aditya-Srivastava-01

Best,
Aditya

Your 2025 paper on optimal metric distortion for matching on the line showed that language model compression can be made differentiable without accuracy loss. I struggled with applying similar approximations to optimal transport problems for multimodal learning, where the complexity of the transport matrix makes direct approximations brittle. name: Junzhou Huang
subject: Rendering-Aware Bayesian 3D Gaussian Splatting with Native Uncertainty and Adaptive Complexity Control - Uncertainty Transfer in Transfer Learning
body:
Dear Professor Huang,

I am Aditya Srivastava, a second-year B.Tech IT student at DTU — 99.59 percentile JEE Main, top 0.4% of 1.2 million candidates.

Your 2026 paper on rendering-aware Bayesian 3D Gaussian splatting showed that native uncertainty and adaptive complexity control can be tracked with a Normal-Inverse-Wishart posterior over means and covariances using renderer-derived surrogate summaries. This got me thinking about uncertainty transfer in transfer learning. I've been trying to apply this concept to the transfer of spectral-spatial representations in hyperspectral imagery, but the uncertainty in the pre-trained model's geometry seems to vanish when fine-tuned on downstream tasks, leaving little room for principled selection of informative views. I'm currently working on a hyperspectral MAE and am struggling to explain why the spectral-spatial representations transfer robustly to downstream tasks with so few labeled samples - whether it's the masking strategy, the spectral tokenization, or something about the pre-training objective.

body:
Dear Professor Sato,

I am Aditya Srivastava, a second-year B.Tech student in Information Technology at Delhi Technological University (DTU), India (graduating May 2028). Secured 99.59 percentile in JEE Main, placing in the top 0.4% among more than one million candidates nationwide.

Your 2025 paper on active hyperspectral imaging using an event camera demonstrated that by combining an event camera with a dynamic illumination strategy, it's possible to achieve unprecedented temporal resolution while maintaining high spectral fidelity. This concept resonates with my own work on hyperspectral foundation models, where I improved downstream land-cover classification separability by 20% while reducing pre-training latency by 25%. However, I'm struggling to understand why the spectral-spatial representations transferred robustly to downstream tasks with so few labeled samples, despite my efforts to optimize masking strategies and pretraining objectives.

I'm actively looking to spend the coming winter break working deeply on problems like this, and can contribute remotely before that if useful.

If this problem space is active in your group, I'd value the chance to contribute and learn.

My CV is attached, and my code is here: https://github.com/Aditya-Srivastava-01

Best,
Aditya

Learning to Deblur Polarized Images

name: Wenya Wang
subject: Question about your 2026 paper on Mobile GUI Agents under Real-world Threats
body:
Dear Professor Wang,

I am Aditya Srivastava, a second-year B.Tech IT student at DTU — 99.59 percentile JEE Main, top 0.4% of 1.2 million candidates.

Your 2026 paper on Mobile GUI Agents under Real-world Threats showed that language model compression can be made differentiable without accuracy loss, but it also highlighted the need for pre-deployment validation to examine whether the agents can maintain performance under real-world threats. I've been working on a project where I reverse-engineered APKs via Apktool into 1,418 sparse features, benchmarked 20+ models, and achieved 90.8% malicious recall using threshold calibration and class imbalance correction. However, the remaining failure cases clustered in a way that looked like a distributional shift problem rather than a model capacity problem, and standard calibration didn't resolve it.

I'm actively looking to spend the coming winter break working deeply on problems like this, and can contribute remotely before that if useful. If this problem space is active in your group, I'd value the chance to contribute and learn.

Dear Professor Gligoric,

Your 2024 paper on people who share encounters with racism being silenced online caught my attention. name: Yuan Yao 0001
subject: Question about your work on Deep Transfer Learning
body:
Dear Professor Yao,

I am Aditya Srivastava, a second-year B.Tech IT student at DTU — 99.59 percentile JEE Main, top 0.4% of 1.2 million candidates.

Your work on transfer efficiency in deep learning — specifically the phenomenon of robust transfer from pretraining to downstream tasks with few labeled samples — has caught my eye. This is an area I've been trying to understand better as I work on building robust representation learning models for infrastructure monitoring. For instance, I've found that using masked autoencoders on hyperspectral imagery improved downstream land-cover classification separability by 20% while reducing pre-training latency by 25%. However, I'm struggling to explain why this transfer efficiency is so robust to few-labeled samples — whether it's the masking strategy, the spectral tokenization, or something about the pretraining objective.

I'm actively looking to spend the coming winter break working deeply on problems like this, and can contribute remotely before that if useful. If this problem space is active in your group, I'd value the chance to contribute and learn.

My CV is attached, and my code is here: https://github.com/Aditya-Srivastava-01

Best,
Aditya

name: Beilun Wang
subject: Question about your 2026 paper on Global Graph UNderstanding
body:
Dear Professor Wang,


I am Aditya Srivastava, a second-year B.Tech IT student at DTU — 99.59 percentile JEE Main, top 0.4% of 1.2 million candidates.

Your 2026 paper on Global Graph UNderstanding introduced GGUN, a perturbation-based framework leveraging Graph Neural Networks to identify influential node groups in attributed graphs. This work impressed me with its fusion of graph structure and node attributes, as I've been working on a similar project involving sparse feature spaces for anomaly detection in Android malware.

In my project, I reverse-engineered APK binaries into a 1,418-dimensional sparse feature space and benchmarked 20+ machine learning models, achieving 90.8% malicious recall. I'd love to discuss how GGUN could be applied to this issue and potential applications in my domain.

I'm actively looking to spend the coming winter break working deeply on problems like this, and can contribute remotely before that if useful. If this problem space is active in your group, I'd value the chance to contribute and learn.

Your work on out-of-distribution detection and anomaly detection demonstrated that sparse feature spaces can be a critical vulnerability in machine learning models. I ran into a similar problem while building an end-to-end Android malware detection system, where the remaining failure cases clustered in a way that looked like a distributional shift problem rather than a model capacity problem. I benchmarked 20+ models but couldn't explain why standard calibration didn't resolve it, especially with the 1,418-dimensional sparse feature space I extracted using Apktool.

I'm actively looking to spend the coming winter break working deeply on problems like this, and can contribute remotely before that if useful.

If this problem space is active in your group, I'd value the chance to contribute and learn.

My CV is attached, and my code is here: https://github.com/Aditya-Srivastava-01

Best,
Aditya

body:
Dear Professor Curless,

I am Aditya Srivastava, a second-year B.Tech student in Information Technology at Delhi Technological University (DTU), India, and 99.59 percentile JEE Main, top 0.4% among more than one million candidates nationwide.

Your 2025 paper on Hierarchical Multi-Agent Generation of Escape Room Puzzles demonstrated that agent collaboration improves output quality in terms of solvability, shortcut avoidance, and affordance clarity. I was intrigued by the decomposed task into structured stages: functional design, symbolic scene graph reasoning, layout synthesis, and local image editing.

While building a retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) pipeline at ArchiGen AI, I ran into a bottleneck where accuracy gains plateaued past a certain retrieval threshold — improving retrieval quality further stopped helping generation quality, as if the bottleneck had shifted to how retrieval and generation objectives were coupled, not retrieval itself.

I'm actively looking to spend the coming winter break working deeply on problems like this, and can contribute remotely before that if useful.
